_meta.description: Dual-extrusion and multi-material compatibility rules. Which materials can be printed together, support dissolution methods, and interface adhesion.

support_pairs.dissolution_method: Submerge in warm water (40-50C) for 2-12 hours. Agitation or ultrasonic bath speeds dissolution.
support_pairs.notes: PVA is the gold standard water-soluble support for PLA. Keep PVA filament bone-dry in a sealed container with desiccant — even brief humidity exposure causes jams and poor dissolution.

support_pairs.dissolution_method: Submerge in warm water (40-50C) for 2-12 hours. Agitation or ultrasonic bath speeds dissolution.
support_pairs.notes: Identical behavior to PLA + PVA. PLA Plus prints at the same temps as PLA, so PVA is not thermally stressed.

support_pairs.dissolution_method: Submerge in warm water (40-50C) for 2-12 hours.
support_pairs.notes: Works well, but PVA contact areas may leave a slightly matte mark on the silk finish. Orient support contact away from visible surfaces.

support_pairs.dissolution_method: Submerge in d-Limonene at room temperature for 12-24 hours. Use in a well-ventilated area.
support_pairs.notes: HIPS is the standard dissolvable support for ABS. Both print at similar temps and share styrene chemistry, so they bond and release cleanly. Limonene is reusable until saturated.

support_pairs.dissolution_method: Submerge in warm water (40-50C) for 4-16 hours. Takes longer than PLA+PVA due to denser interface.
support_pairs.notes: PVA degrades at PETG print temps (230-250C). Use 2-3 dense interface layers to insulate PVA from the PETG nozzle heat. Reduce PETG temp to low end of range (230C) when co-printing. Workable but not ideal.

support_pairs.dissolution_method: Submerge in warm water (40-50C) for 6-24 hours.
support_pairs.notes: Temperature mismatch is too large — nylon prints at 240-270C while PVA degrades above 210C. PVA chars and jams at nylon temps. Only feasible with very low nylon temps and thick interface layers. Not recommended for production use.

support_pairs.dissolution_method: Submerge in d-Limonene at room temperature for 12-24 hours. Use in a well-ventilated area. Limonene is reusable until saturated.
support_pairs.notes: ASA and HIPS are both styrene-based, so they bond and release similarly to ABS+HIPS. Slightly less clean separation than ABS+HIPS due to ASA's acrylic component, but still a reliable pairing for dissolvable supports.

support_pairs.dissolution_method: Submerge in d-Limonene at room temperature for 12-24 hours.
support_pairs.notes: PLA and HIPS have incompatible print temp ranges and bed adhesion requirements. PLA prefers 50-65C bed, HIPS needs 90-110C. The interface bond is weak and inconsistent. PVA is a far better soluble support choice for PLA.

support_pairs.dissolution_method: Not viable — HIPS degrades before dissolution is useful.
support_pairs.notes: PC prints at 260-310C which is far above HIPS's softening point. HIPS deforms, chars, and fuses to the PC during printing. Not a viable support pairing. Use breakaway supports or dedicated high-temp support materials instead.

support_pairs.dissolution_method: Submerge in warm water (40-50C) for 4-12 hours.
support_pairs.notes: PVA works as soluble support for TPU but the interface can bond too aggressively — PVA tends to embed into the soft TPU surface. Use 2-3 interface layers with reduced density. Dissolution may leave a rough texture on the TPU contact surface that needs trimming.

pla_plus.notes: Same polymer family. PLA Plus is just PLA with impact modifiers — they bond seamlessly at identical print temps.

silk_pla.notes: Same PLA base. Silk additives don't affect inter-material adhesion.

wood_pla.notes: Wood filler slightly reduces interface strength vs pure PLA, but still bonds well.

cf_pla.notes: Carbon fibers reduce interface bonding area slightly. Use 2-3 interface layers for best results.

petg.notes: Different polymer families with incompatible shrinkage rates. Poor chemical bonding at the interface. Parts delaminate under stress.

abs.notes: Very different print temps (60C+ gap) and shrinkage rates. ABS warps and pulls away from PLA during cooling. Do not combine.

tpu.notes: Works for soft-touch grips on rigid PLA parts. Adhesion is mechanical rather than chemical — use interlocking geometry at the interface for reliability.

pva.notes: PVA is designed as soluble support for PLA. Bonds well during printing, dissolves cleanly in water.

asa.notes: Same styrene-acrylonitrile family. Virtually interchangeable — similar print temps, shrinkage, and chemistry. ASA adds UV resistance to outdoor-facing surfaces.

hips.notes: Standard support pairing. HIPS bonds well during printing and dissolves in d-Limonene. Both are styrene-based.

pc_abs.notes: PC-ABS contains ABS, so they share partial chemistry. PC-ABS prints hotter — use ABS at the high end of its temp range.

pla.notes: Very different print temps and shrinkage rates. ABS warps and pulls away from PLA during cooling.

petg.notes: Different polymer families. Poor chemical bonding despite overlapping print temps. Interface delaminates under thermal cycling or stress.

tpu.notes: Works for overmolded soft-touch grips on rigid ABS structures. Needs careful temp management — print ABS first, then TPU at lower temps to avoid softening the ABS. Use interlocking geometry at the interface.

asa_plus.notes: Same base polymer. ASA Plus adds impact modifiers — they bond seamlessly.

abs.notes: Same styrene family. Interchangeable in multi-material prints.

hips.notes: HIPS works as dissolvable support for ASA just as it does for ABS.

petg_cf.notes: Same PETG base. CF variant has slightly reduced interface bonding due to fiber content but works well with interface layers.

cf_petg.notes: Same PETG base with carbon fiber. Use 2-3 interface layers for best adhesion.

pet_cf.notes: PET-CF is closely related to PETG-CF. Similar enough chemistry for decent interface bonding.

abs.notes: Different polymer families. Despite similar print temps, they don't bond chemically and delaminate under stress.

tpu.notes: Temperature mismatch and no chemical bonding. TPU is too flexible to form a reliable interface with rigid PETG.

nylon.notes: Different polymer families with incompatible shrinkage. Nylon's high moisture absorption also destabilizes the interface over time.

pla.notes: Different polymer families. Poor chemical bonding at the interface.

abs.notes: PC-ABS blends exist commercially, proving these polymers are compatible. PC prints hotter — keep ABS at its high end (250C+) and PC at its low end.

pc_abs.notes: PC-ABS is literally a blend of these two materials. They bond exceptionally well.

pla.notes: PC prints at 260-310C which is well above PLA's melting point (~180C). PLA will completely melt and degrade while PC is printing. Absolutely incompatible.

cf_nylon.notes: Same nylon base. CF filler reduces interface area slightly but bonding is reliable.

petg.notes: Different polymer families with incompatible shrinkage and moisture behavior.

pla.notes: Completely incompatible. Nylon prints at 240-270C which softens PLA. Wildly different shrinkage rates (nylon ~1.5% vs PLA ~0.3%), opposite bed adhesion needs, and zero chemical affinity. Do not attempt.
Works for soft-touch grips on rigid PLA parts. Design interlocking geometry at the interface rather than relying on adhesion alone.

petg.notes: Temperature mismatch and no chemical affinity. TPU oozes badly at PETG print temps.

pla.notes: Same PLA base polymer. CF filler slightly reduces bonding area but still reliable.

petg.notes: Same PETG base. Good compatibility with minor interface adhesion reduction from fibers.

nylon.notes: Same nylon base. CF reduces interface bonding area but chemistry is compatible.

abs.notes: PC-ABS contains ABS component — good chemical affinity.

polycarbonate.notes: PC-ABS is a blend of these two. Natural compatibility.

general_rules: Materials with similar print temps (within 20C) generally co-print well — the closer the temps, the less oozing and degradation from the idle nozzle.
Same polymer family materials bond best at interfaces: PLA variants with PLA variants, styrene family (ABS/ASA/HIPS) together, PETG variants together, nylon variants together.
Amorphous + crystalline polymer combinations usually have poor interface adhesion due to fundamentally different solidification behavior and shrinkage patterns.
Soluble support materials (PVA, HIPS) must be stored in sealed containers with desiccant until use — even moderate humidity exposure causes jams, poor print quality, and incomplete dissolution.
Interface/transition layers (2-3 dense layers of shared perimeter) significantly improve adhesion between dissimilar materials by increasing contact area and mechanical interlocking.

Purge tower volume should increase proportionally with the temperature differential between materials — a 10C gap needs minimal purge, but 40C+ gaps need 3-4x the default purge volume to prevent contamination.
Materials with very different shrinkage rates (e.g., ABS at 0.8% vs PLA at 0.3%) will delaminate at interfaces during cooling, especially on large parts.
Flexible + rigid material combinations work best when the flexible material is the outer layer or grip surface — rigid inner structure with flexible overmold is mechanically superior to the reverse.
Carbon fiber and glass fiber filled materials have reduced interface adhesion compared to their unfilled base polymer due to fibers disrupting the polymer chain entanglement at the boundary.
Dual extrusion with materials more than 40C apart in print temperature often causes the lower-temp material to degrade, char, or ooze excessively while the higher-temp material is printing.
